story:
A warm spring rain taps at the windows and wakes Emily from a long nap. She likes the rain; it always makes her feel clean, fresh, and shiny new. Winter, though, she hates. Emily remembers the bleak day last November when she first came to St. Christopher’s, shivering at the memory of a harrowing ride through snow and doubt. The driver wasn’t sure she would make it, but she did. After a careful examination, they wheeled her to a quiet, comfortable corner. It was warm there. Peaceful. She settled into the hush and lost track of time.

When she wakes again, someone new has been brought in beside her: a sixteen-year-old with smooth cream coloring and chestnut accents, battered and bandaged but striking. Emily smiles. Hi. I’m Emily. The newcomer bristles, defensive and silent. Emily notices the nasty bruise. The girl stares past her, hard and hollow, and Emily’s heart aches at the sight. So young to be so guarded. Emily has seen this sort of mistreatment before. It always sickens her. She has been fortunate; in her forty-five years she’s known mostly gentleness, care, and respect.

I’m sorry; you look like someone who could use a friend, she offers. After a tense quiet, the teen relents, blinking back a tear. I’m not used to anyone being nice. I’m Ginger. They talk. Ginger is scared. It’s her first time here. What’s wrong with you, if you don’t mind? You seem awfully calm for someone whose driver wasn’t sure she would make it through that ride in the snow last November. Emily doesn’t. It depends on who you ask, she says with calm honesty. Some say it’s minor. Some say it’s terminal. I don’t think I’ll be leaving through the front door.

Ginger is startled by her frankness. But you’re still so beautiful. Emily chuckles softly. Only on the outside. It’s what’s inside that counts. I’ve been around the block, and some of my parts are just worn out. The thought doesn’t frighten her. In a way, she tells Ginger, she will leave. She’s a donor. Her usable parts will live on. A kind of immortality.

The idea stirs something tender in Ginger. All I’ve ever had was my looks, she admits. I’ve never felt seen on the inside. I went unwanted at the start and got passed from hand to hand. No security. Just what others wanted from me. As she speaks, Emily’s affection for the battered girl deepens. Rest, she whispers. Everything will work out. For the first time, Ginger feels she truly has a friend. She makes a silent wish to stay with Emily forever, and falls asleep.

By morning, Emily is gone. Ginger knows she won’t see her again, yet she feels her presence close. Two men arrive and wheel Ginger into a larger room. With time and work, she’ll be better than ever, they agree.
